exemplar.title: To what extent is it more impactful for the Civil Society HUB to redirect their focus on food supply in war situations?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is it more impactful for the Civil Society HUB to redirect their focus on food supply in war situations?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear identification and repeated reference to the key concept of change
Good initial integration of change in the discussion of CSHâs shift from evacuation to food supply
Consistent linkage of STEEPLE factors and Ansoff choices back to the concept
weaknesses: Integration is not fully seamlessâthe concept drives only portions of the report
Lacks an explicit theoretical definition or framework of âchangeâ
Some sections (cost tables, STEEPLE subsections) treat change as background rather than analytical thread
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Bandâ3 performance: the student shows good understanding and clear analytical thinking around the concept of change, with consistent ties to CSH, but falls short of the fully integrated analysis required for higher bands.

strengths: Five distinct and relevant sources meet IA document limits
weaknesses: Most sources are used superficially and contribute little depth to argumentation
References lack peerâreviewed or official reports to strengthen evidence
Bibliography has incomplete details for some footnotes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Bandâ2 performance: while the number of documents is appropriate, their relevance to the analysis is superficial and lacks depth, aligning with a midâband mark of 2/4.

strengths: Selection of standard tools (STEEPLE, Ansoff Matrix, cost comparison)
Clear relevance of tools to the research question
weaknesses: Analysis using some tools remains mainly descriptive (e.g., Porter's Generic Strategies listed but unused)
Relevance of certain applications (e.g., cost data interpretation) is not always fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Bandâ3 in tool selection and relevance is evident, but effectiveness is moderate; the descriptive nature and unused tools warrant a slightly reduced mark of 2/4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Correct budget and beneficiary data support the costâefficiency argument
Some STEEPLEâbased analysis of contextual factors
weaknesses: Data selection is limited and not triangulated or critically tested
Analysis stops at surface level without sensitivity testing or deeper evaluation
Several sections are incomplete or lack citations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Bandâ2 performance: the student shows limited but present use of data and analysis. The superficial depth and incomplete components justify 2/5.

strengths: Conclusions are generally consistent with presented evidence
The research question is explicitly addressed
weaknesses: Conclusion merely restates earlier points without synthesizing new insights
Limited evidence base and narrow analytical scope make the conclusion underdeveloped
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Bandâ2 qualities exist, but the superficial nature and lack of synthesis reduce the mark to 1/3 for conclusions.

strengths: Logical sequence with clear table of contents
Research question is clearly stated and guides the structure
weaknesses: Introduction is abrupt and lacks sufficient framing
Minor formatting lapses in headings and subâheadings detract from readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Overall organization is present, but structural lapses (abrupt opening, inconsistent headings) lead to a reduced mark of 1/2.

strengths: All IA required elements are included (title page, word count, bibliography)
Inâtext citations and footnotes are present
weaknesses: Inconsistent application of citation formatting and quotation marks
Footnote formatting errors and missing visual diagrams reduce clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite meeting basic IA presentation requirements (Bandâ2), formatting inconsistencies warrant a reduced mark of 1/2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA follows a logical sequence (introduction, tools, conclusion) and provides a table of contents.  Headings and sub-headings guide the reader; despite minor formatting lapses, overall organization meets IB expectations, warranting the top structural band (2 marks).

Sources are pertinent to the topic but used largely for factual background.  Only the CSH internal document feeds directly into argumentation; others are mentioned superficially.  Depth and triangulation are limited, so the work aligns with âgenerally relevant but lack depthâ (Band 2).

The student includes a title page with word count, a contents table, section headings, in-text citations, and a bibliography, satisfying all IA presentation requirements; therefore, full marks for this strand are appropriate.

Five distinct sources are cited (Dalal, World Bank, Bilali & Ben Hassen, CSH internal document, Social Work Hub).  This satisfies the 3-5 range required and remains within IA limits, meriting the mid-band score.

STEEPLE factors are applied directly to CSH, and the Ansoff discussion links strategy choice to resource constraints in a war zone.  Relevance is clear, but analysis sometimes remains descriptive (e.g., Porter's tool unused, limited numerical interpretation), so effectiveness is moderate rather than strong, placing it in Band 3.
Data are sparseâmainly budget figures and beneficiary counts.  They are correct and used, but not triangulated or critically tested.  This fits "superficial selection and use of data" (Band 2).

The analysis explains why food supply could reach more beneficiaries and runs a brief STEEPLE-based argument, yet depth is limited: calculations stop at averages, no sensitivity testing appears, and counter-arguments are thin.  Thus, the quality matches Band 2, indicating limited but present analysis.

The research question is answered explicitly in the conclusion, yet earlier sections wander and do not always build a coherent chain to that answer.  This constitutes a partial but not fully developed response, appropriate for Band 2.

The student selects several recognised toolsâSTEEPLE, Ansoff Matrix, cost-efficiency comparison (and Porter's Generic Strategies listed, though not developed).  This constitutes an adequate range, consistent with Band 3.

The conclusion that food supply is more impactful is generally in line with the evidence presented (budget table, beneficiary numbers), but because the evidence base itself is narrow and some STEEPLE claims are not evidenced, consistency is only partial, aligning with Band 2.

The submission maintains a consistent focus on Civil Society Hub, linking most pointsâSTEEPLE factors, Ansoff choices, cost dataâto the organisationâs decisions and performance.  Yet several comparisons drift to the World Food Programme or generic wartime issues without always looping back to CSH, so the analysis is clear but not fully integrated throughout, fitting Band 3 rather than Bands 4-5.

The student clearly identifies the key concept of change and refers to it several times (e.g., the shift from evacuation to food supply, the need to adapt strategies to war conditions).  However, the concept drives only portions of the discussion; many sections (STEEPLE subsections, cost tables) treat change as a background remark rather than the analytical thread running through the whole report.  This demonstrates "good understanding with clear analytical thinking" but falls short of the full, seamless integration required for Band 5.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and focused, guiding the investigation effectively.
The key concept âChangeâ is named but lacks an explicit definition or theoretical framing. Consider defining how âchangeâ informs each analytical tool choice.
All required IA frontâpage elementsâincluding word countâare present and correctly formatted.
The table of contents includes all major sections, aiding navigation and reflecting a coherent structure.
The opening of the introduction is abrupt and lacks context. Consider adding a clear background to frame why CSHâs shift merits investigation.
The initial description of CSHâs evacuation and food supply change shows good integration of the key concept in context.
This paragraph describes CSHâs change but lacks citation linking to evidence. Add a source to support the statement that the Kharkiv food project failed.
Comparative discussion of WFP is useful context but needs explicit linkage to how CSH could adapt similar localâpartner strategies.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Quotation marks are inconsistently applied (e.g., missing closing marks). Review citation formatting per IA style guidelines.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent (e.g., orphan â<sup>3</sup>â). Standardize per APA or MLA guidelines.
The summary of constant change effectively ties back to the key concept but could engage deeper evaluation of CSHâs adaptability.
The STEEPLE introduction explains scope but referring to all seven factors then omitting two could confuse the reader.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The social factor discussion is descriptive but lacks deeper analysis of its impact on CSHâs operations.
The economic factor section ends midâsentence (âFinancial statements would beâ¦â). Complete the thought and explain its relevance.
The environmental factor insight on supplier shortages is valid but could be strengthened by data on local food stock levels or supplier closures.
The political factor mentions border reliance but lacks evidence of Polish policy changes. Consider citing official border statistics or reports.
The budget table presents correct figures clearlyâgood use of data to support the costâefficiency argument.
The costâefficiency analysis lacks explicit calculation steps. Show your formulas (e.g., $$\frac{Total\ Budget}{Number\ of\ Meals}$$) to enhance transparency.
The Ansoff Matrix is described accurately but no diagram is provided. Including the matrix visuals would enhance clarity and application.
The link between market penetration and CSHâs funding model is mentioned but lacks critical evaluation of risk versus benefit.
The reference to CSHâs adaptability highlights change but abruptly cuts off. Complete the analysis on how STEEPLE challenges influenced final recommendations.
Bibliography includes core sources but is heavily webâbased. Incorporate peerâreviewed journals or official reports to deepen evidence base.
The bibliography list lacks a consistent formatting style; ensure uniform citation formatting across all entries.
Porterâs Generic Strategies is listed in the contents but not applied in the analysis. Either develop this tool or remove it from the structure.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Citation of âSocial Work Hubâ in the footnote lacks full reference details; ensure each source appears in bibliography.
The beneficiary comparison is a strong quantitative evaluation, supporting the recommendation effectively.
The conclusion reiterates points already made without synthesizing new insights. Aim to draw out implications or recommendations beyond restatement.
